data.title: What’s next for Tesla after a disappointing EV delivery report
data.description:
What is next for Tesla after a disappointing Q1 2026 report?

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has faced a challenging start to 2026, with shares falling approximately 21.5% year to date as of early April. The selloff was accelerated by a disappointing first-quarter production and delivery report, which revealed a significant inventory build of 50,363 vehicles and a dramatic 38.9% miss in energy storage expectations.

Still, while the immediate reaction to the quarterly figures has been negative, analysts remain divided on whether the current valuation represents a buying opportunity or a warning sign of further declines.

What Do Seeking Alpha Analysts Say About Tesla’s Future?

Bulls pointed to the long-term “optionality” inherent in Tesla’s non-automotive ventures, specifically highlighting progress in Full Self-Driving (FSD), the Robotaxi initiative, and the Optimus robot. They also noted pockets of international growth, such as untapped demand catalysts in South Korea, and suggested that the risk-reward ratio is becoming favorable as the stock becomes “cheap” relative to its future potential in AI and energy.

Bears, however, highlighted structural issues in the core automotive business, including narrowing competitive moats and intensifying pressure from rivals like BYD (BYDDF). They pointed to the massive discrepancy between production and deliveries, leading to a concerning rise in inventory, and argued that a trailing P/E ratio of over 300x is unjustifiable given the current margin compression and brand deterioration.

Here’s a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

  • The Techie, Seeking Alpha Analyst, Rating: Buy: “Tesla stock is experiencing a sharp selloff, down 21.5% YTD, as the broader market pulls back on geopolitical uncertainty. I’m upgrading Tesla to a cautious Buy at current levels and think the risk-reward is favorable if the geopolitical and tariff headwinds ease.” - Tesla Stock: It’s Cheap Again (Rating Upgrade)

  • Amrita Roy, Investing Group Leader, Rating: Buy: “Tesla faces near-term headwinds in its core automotive segment, but I see 2026 as a ‘prove it’ year for its AI and robotics vision… I plan to add to TSLA shares at $330 and $273, maintaining an underweight portfolio allocation, as I expect short-term volatility, but not to the degree that Wall Street analysts suggest.” - Tesla Won’t Crash Anytime Soon

  • Forward Analytics, Financial Analyst, Rating: Sell: “TSLA faces structural delivery declines, margin compression, brand deterioration, and intensifying competition, notably from BYD… My DCF scenarios project 14% to 76% downside from current levels, even under optimistic assumptions.” - Tesla: Three Scenarios, Zero Upside

  • Bill Maurer, Market Enthusiast, Rating: Bearish: “TSLA’s valuation remains extremely high at 183x 2026 earnings, far exceeding both traditional automakers and tech giants… the energy storage business fell dramatically short of analyst expectations.” - Tesla: Weak Start To 2026

  • HF Analyst, Equity Research, Rating: Bearish: “Inventory build is getting worse with 50,363 vehicles… TSLA’s competitive EV moat has narrowed, raising questions about its long-term market dominance and valuation.” - Tesla Q1 2026 Deliveries Strengthen The Bearish Outlook

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

Tesla currently carries a cautious rating from Seeking Alpha’s quant system, reflecting the disconnect between its high-growth narrative and recent operational misses. While the company retains its status as a tech leader, its quantitative scores have been weighed down by delivery declines and valuation concerns.

Valuation: The company receives a poor grade in valuation, with a trailing P/E ratio of 335.12x that far exceeds the sector median and traditional automotive peers.

Growth: Growth metrics have come under pressure, receiving a low grade following a Q1 2026 delivery report that showed vehicle deliveries falling well below analyst expectations.

Profitability: Despite margin compression, Tesla maintains a relatively strong grade in profitability compared to the sector median, though analysts warn this is at risk due to ongoing demand-stimulating discounts.

Momentum: Momentum carries a weak grade, as the stock has plummeted 21.5% YTD, severely underperforming the S&P 500 and other members of the “Mag 7.”

Earnings Revisions: The company is receiving a downward revision grade, as analysts lower Q1 and full-year 2026 estimates following the weak energy storage and delivery results.

What’s the Latest News on Tesla?

Tesla (TSLA) recently released its Q1 2026 production and delivery report, which has served as a primary catalyst for recent bearish sentiment. The report revealed that production significantly exceeded deliveries, leading to an inventory surplus of 50,363 vehicles. Furthermore, the company’s energy storage business, a key pillar of its non-automotive growth story, missed analyst estimates by a staggering 38.9%.

The company is also grappling with intensifying competition in the electric vehicle space, particularly from BYD (BYDDY), which continues to challenge Tesla’s market share. This competitive pressure, combined with expiring EV incentives and geopolitical uncertainty involving tariffs, has forced Tesla to utilize demand-stimulating discounts that threaten its long-term margin profile.

Looking ahead, investors are focusing on the company’s “prove it” year in AI and robotics. While core automotive metrics are struggling, the market is closely watching for tangible progress in the Robotaxi and Optimus programs, as these segments are increasingly viewed as the primary justification for the company’s $1.35 trillion market capitalization.

data.title: Industrial stocks hit fresh highs as ceasefire rally lifts infrastructure names
data.description:
Shares of a broad swath of U.S. industrial companies surged to 52-week highs on Wednesday, as markets rallied following a ceasefire agreement between the United States and Iran, easing geopolitical tensions that had weighed on investor sentiment.

The gains were particularly pronounced among companies tied to infrastructure, electrification and defense-adjacent end markets, highlighting how investors are rotating into businesses with both cyclical upside and long-term structural demand drivers.

Among the notable movers hitting fresh highs were Babcock & Wilcox (BW), BWX Technologies (BWXT), Comfort Systems USA (FIX), Curtiss-Wright (CW), ESCO Technologies (ESE), ITT Inc. (ITT), MasTec (MTZ), Modine Manufacturing (MOD), MYR Group (MYRG), Powell Industries (POWL), Preformed Line Products (PLP), Spire Global (SPIR), Vertiv Holdings (VRT) and Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies (WAB).

Market rally with a clear tilt

The ceasefire catalyst helped drive a broad risk-on move, but the concentration of new highs within this group points to a more targeted theme. Many of these companies sit at the intersection of three powerful trends: the buildout of AI-driven data centers, electrification of the grid and increased geopolitical focus on domestic infrastructure and defense resilience.

Investors appear to be rewarding firms with exposure to large-scale capital spending cycles that are already underway and, in many cases, accelerating.

AI infrastructure emerges as a unifying thread

A key common denominator is exposure to the physical infrastructure behind artificial intelligence. Companies such as Vertiv, Modine, Comfort Systems and Powell Industries provide cooling systems, power management and electrical equipment essential for hyperscale data centers.

As major technology companies race to expand capacity for AI workloads, demand for these systems has surged, driving strong revenue growth, expanding margins and record backlogs across the sector.

Electrification, grid investment add another tailwind

Another shared theme is electrification. Firms like MYR Group, MasTec and Preformed Line Products are leveraged to transmission upgrades, renewable integration and broader grid modernization efforts.

These trends have been reinforced by policy support and rising electricity demand, particularly from data centers and industrial reshoring.

Defense and energy security in focus

A subset of the group also benefits from heightened defense and energy security spending. BWX Technologies and Curtiss-Wright, for example, have exposure to naval nuclear systems and defense components, while Babcock & Wilcox is tied to energy and environmental technologies.

Even with the ceasefire, investors appear to be pricing in a sustained period of elevated spending in these areas.

Momentum meets fundamentals

The surge to 52-week highs reflects not only improving sentiment but also strong underlying performance. Many of these companies have reported accelerating earnings growth, expanding cash flow and rapidly growing backlogs, signaling durable demand rather than short-term cyclical spikes.

The combination of easing geopolitical risk and structural growth drivers has created a powerful backdrop for industrial names that are no longer viewed as purely cyclical, but as critical enablers of the next phase of global infrastructure investment.

Stocks hitting 52-week highs (alphabetical):

  • Babcock & Wilcox (BW)
  • BWX Technologies (BWXT)
  • Comfort Systems USA (FIX)
  • Curtiss-Wright (CW)
  • ESCO Technologies (ESE)
  • ITT Inc. (ITT)
  • MasTec (MTZ)
  • Modine Manufacturing (MOD)
  • MYR Group (MYRG)
  • Powell Industries (POWL)
  • Preformed Line Products (PLPC)
  • Spire Global (SPIR)
  • Vertiv Holdings (VRT)
  • Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies (WAB)

The breadth of the move suggests that, while the ceasefire may have sparked the rally, the underlying story is much bigger: a multi-year investment cycle in AI, energy and infrastructure that continues to reshape the industrial landscape.

data.title: Iran may exit ceasefire over Israeli attacks on Lebanon - reports
data.description:
Iran signaled Wednesday it may withdraw from the current ceasefire agreement if Israel continues its attacks on Lebanon, according to a source speaking to Tasnim news agency, which is believed to have ties to Iran’s paramilitary Revolutionary Guard, the Associated Press reported.

The source indicated that Iran is actively reviewing the possibility of exiting the fragile ceasefire framework, emphasizing that a halt to fighting on all fronts—including against Lebanon’s Hezbollah—had been agreed upon by the U.S. as part of a two-week truce proposal.

According to the source, Israel has carried out “clear violations of the ceasefire” with attacks on Lebanon since this morning, as the Strait of Hormuz remained largely blocked. In response, Iran’s armed forces are reportedly identifying potential targets.

In a pointed warning, the source stated that if the U.S. cannot restrain its “rabid dog” in the region, Iran would “exceptionally help it do so—by force.”

Earlier on Wednesday, Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif urged all parties in the Middle East conflict to respect the agreed-upon ceasefire, warning that reported violations are undermining diplomatic efforts.

data.title: Barclays upgrades DNB Bank on upside potential to consensus NII
data.description:
DNB Bank (DNBBF) (DNBBY) was upgraded to Overweight on upside potential to consensus NII.

"We see clear upside potential to consensus NII even in a flat-rate environment and a more defensible competitive position in Norway. Our FY26-28 EPS estimates are 4-8% above consensus," said analysts Namita Samtani, Raghav Agrawal, and Krishnendra Dubey.

Nordic banks' price-to-equity multiples have re-rated year-to-date. However, Barclays is selective given the trade-off between potential rate upside and competitive conditions in Sweden (constrained loan growth, rich deposit margins at risk, and fee pressure), according to the research note.

The P/E multiples have re-rated +6% on average vs. a -3% de-rating for EU banks. Most re-ratings occurred before the Iran/U.S. conflict, noted the report.

data.title: Firefly Aerospace rallies after updating on its moon imaging project
data.description:
Firefly Aerospace (FLY) announced a new collaboration with Nvidia (NVDA) to enable rapid on-orbit processing in lunar orbit for the company's Ocula Moon imaging service. As part of the collaboration, an Nvidia (NVDA) Jetson module was embedded on high-resolution Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory telescopes and delivered to Firefly's (FLY) spacecraft facility ahead of integration on its Elytra orbital vehicle.

"Ocula will be powered by the world’s leading edge AI processor. This capability allows us to layer on our SciTec AI software as the ‘brains’ that give customers real-time, data-driven insights from the Moon," highlighted Firefly Aerospace (FLY) CEO Jason Kim.

The Ocula service will be activated onboard Elytra as part of Firefly's (FLY) second mission to the Moon, which is targeted to launch no earlier than late 2026. Elytra will first serve as a transfer vehicle and long-haul communications relay for Firefly’s Blue Ghost lander. Elytra will then remain operational in lunar orbit for approximately five years, capturing continuous imagery for the Ocula service, supporting advanced lunar surface mapping, mineral detection, and reconnaissance.

Firefly (FLY) said its AI-powered software will further enable advanced space domain awareness in lunar orbit. Following Blue Ghost Mission 2, Firefly (FLY) is on contract to deploy two additional Elytra vehicles to lunar orbit as part of Blue Ghost Mission 3 and Mission 4, enabling faster revisit times for space domain awareness, lunar surface mapping, and resource detection.

Shares of Firefly Aerospace (FLY) rallied 9.8% in early afternoon trading and carved out a new 2026 high earlier in the session.

data.title: Inhibrx gains as Stifel issues new Buy based on lead assets
data.description:
Inhibrx Biosciences (INBX) traded higher on Wednesday after Stifel launched its coverage of the cancer drug developer with a Buy recommendation and a $150 price target, citing the potential of its lead assets, ozekibart and INBRX-106.

Analyst Dara Azar noted that ozekibart and INBRX-106 are designed to target DR5 and OX40, respectively, which he identified as two of the most highly sought-after receptors in oncology.

The analyst estimates $5.3B - $5.8B in risk-unadjusted sales for the candidates, a projection he said "remains largely unpriced at current levels even after appropriate risk adjustments.”

INBRX-106 is a hexavalent agonist targeted at head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, and ozekibart is a tetravalent agonist targeted at colorectal cancer and a rare cancer called chondrosarcoma.

“We think this is only the visible portion of the investment case," the analyst wrote, adding that "there is an emerging or theoretical much larger upside tied to these assets’ broader utility and the platform that could lift that peak estimate substantially."

data.title: Comfort Systems hits 52-week high as ceasefire rally lifts industrial names
data.description:
Shares of Comfort Systems USA (FIX) surged as much as 8.6% on Wednesday, touching a fresh 52-week high as broader markets rebounded following news of a ceasefire agreement between the United States and Iran.

The move adds to a remarkable run for the mechanical and electrical services provider, whose stock has climbed roughly 390% over the past 12 months, making it one of the standout performers in the industrial sector.

AI boom fuels demand

The rally underscores investor enthusiasm for companies tied to the infrastructure buildout behind artificial intelligence. Comfort Systems (FIX) has emerged as a key beneficiary as hyperscale technology companies accelerate construction of data centers, requiring complex heating, ventilation, cooling and electrical systems.

That demand has translated into rapid growth and a swelling backlog, positioning the company as a critical player in the physical layer of the AI economy.

Backlog signals sustained momentum

In February, the company said its backlog reached $11.94 billion at the end of 2025, up from $9.38 billion at the end of the third quarter and nearly double the $5.99 billion reported a year earlier. On a same-store basis, backlog expanded to $11.58 billion from $5.99 billion, highlighting strong underlying demand across its markets.

With geopolitical tensions easing, at least temporarily, and structural demand from AI infrastructure still building, investors appear increasingly willing to bid up companies like Comfort Systems (FIX) that sit at the intersection of industrial execution and next-generation computing growth.

data.title: Chip and AI-related stocks spring as US and Iran agree to temporary ceasefire
data.description:
The semiconductor sector and AI-related stocks joined the broader market in a Wednesday rally after U.S. President Donald Trump agreed to pause planned strikes on Iran for two weeks, contingent on the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Navitas (NVTS) was leading the rally with a nearly 12% surge by noon trading. Intel (INTC) had leaped 8%. It was revealed on Tuesday that Intel has joined the Terafab project alongside billionaire Elon Musk's companies SpaceX (SPACE), xAI (X.AI), and Tesla (TSLA) to "help refactor silicon fab technology." Intel is also engaged in discussions with Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) for its advanced packaging services.

Broadcom (AVGO), Marvell (MRVL), and AMD (AMD) had all climbed 4%. Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) had jumped 5%. Analog Devices (ADI) was also up 5%. Meanwhile, Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) and Lattice (LSCC) were both up about 7%.

AXT (AXTI), a California-based company that designs, develops, and manufactures compound and single-element semiconductor substrates, had rocketed 17% by noon trading.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX) had surged more than 5%, while the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was up 2.5%.

Semiconductor equipment makers joined the rally as well, as Applied Materials (AMAT) made an 8% leap. The company had also revealed a pair of new chipmaking systems for AI GPUs on Wednesday. Lam Research (LRCX) had jumped 9%, ASML (ASML) had gained 8%, and KLA (KLAC) had climbed 7%.

Networking and optical stocks also made significant gains. Coherent (COHR) had climbed 6%, Corning (GLW) was up 7%, Lumentum (LITE) had increased 7.6%, and Ciena (CIEN) had soared 9%.

AI hyperscalers were also in the green as CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS) had both climbed nearly 5%. The world's most prolific cloud providers also moved higher. Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) was up 3.7%, Amazon (AMZN) had edged up 3.5%, and Microsoft had inched up 1.6%.

data.title: CK Hutchison launches arbitration against Maersk over Panama ports
data.description:
CK Hutchison (CKHUY) (CKHUF) said Wednesday it has started arbitration against A.P. Moeller-Maersk (AMKBY) (AMKAF) after Panama's forced takeover of its two ports in the country.

The arbitration, filed by CK Hutchison's (CKHUY) (CKHUF) Panama Ports local unit, will be heard in London and is separate from its $2B-plus damages claim against the Panamanian government, the company said.

The move escalates a dispute that began in January, when a Panamanian court voided CK Hutchison's (CKHUY) (CKHUF) contracts to run two terminals at either end of the Panama Canal; Panama's government had awarded temporary contracts for Maersk (AMKBY) (AMKAF) subsidiaries and the Mediterranean ‌Shipping ⁠Company to run the terminals.

The dispute also complicated CK Hutchison's (CKHUY) (CKHUF) planned $23B sale of a majority stake in its global ports business to a consortium led by BlackRock and MSC; the plan, announced in March 2025, angered the Chinese government, which viewed it as bowing to U.S. pressure.
